content.text: An eval run produces a number. That number is not an answer — it is a question. What changed? Is the change real or noise? Which slice of the suite drove it? What does the model actually do on the failing cases? Interpreting results correctly means walking through a structured analysis before acting on any score.
Step 1 — Read the Overall Score in Context

The first number you see is the aggregate score across all test cases. It tells you whether the run met the threshold and whether it improved or regressed relative to the last run. Before reading anything into the score, check two preconditions:
Suite hash is the same.

Comparing scores across different suites is invalid. If the hash changed — cases were added, removed, or relabeled — re-baseline: run the new suite against the last-known-good model version and use that as the new reference point.
The grader is the same.
A change in the LLM judge model, rubric wording, or normalization logic can produce a score delta with no model change at all. Check the run metadata for grader version before comparing.
With both preconditions satisfied, the score delta is a real signal. What size delta is meaningful?
Delta
Interpretation
Action
+5pp or more
Likely meaningful improvement
Verify cases that improved; check no easy cases were added
+1 to +4pp
Noise zone for suites under 200 cases
Run again; if repeatable, accept as marginal improvement
-1 to -2pp
Noise zone — do not block on this alone
Check sub-group scores; run again to confirm
-3pp or more
Likely real regression
Investigate before shipping; find the failing slice
Suite hash changed
Scores not comparable across runs
Re-baseline first; do not interpret raw delta
Step 2 — Break Down Sub-group Scores

An overall score of 88% can mean the model is uniformly good across all cases, or it can mean the model is perfect on easy cases and completely fails on a critical slice. Sub-group analysis tells you which.

A slice with a pass rate 20+ percentage points below the overall rate is the primary investigation target. The adversarial slice at 30% in the example above is the root cause of the gap — not the edge case slice, which is closer to the overall mean.
Length-Bucket Analysis
Input length is a common confound: models often degrade on long inputs while appearing fine on the overall score because long inputs are a minority of the suite.
Step 3 — Detect Regressions with Statistical Discipline

A regression is a score decrease that is unlikely to be sampling noise. With small suites, individual case variance makes every run slightly different even when nothing changed. Two tools help distinguish noise from signal.
The Two-Run Check

Before investigating any regression, run the eval twice on the same model with the same suite. If the two scores differ by more than 2 percentage points, the suite is too small or the grader too noisy to detect the reported delta reliably. Fix the grader or grow the suite before reading into score differences.
The McNemar Test for Binary Scores
When grader output is binary (pass/fail), McNemar's test checks whether case-level changes between two runs are statistically significant:

from scipy.stats import chi2

def mcnemar_test(before: list[int], after: list[int]) -> dict:
    """
    before, after: lists of 0/1 pass indicators, same order.
    Returns p-value for H0: the two runs are equally good.
    """
    b_pass_a_fail = sum(1 for b, a in zip(before, after) if b == 1 and a == 0)
    b_fail_a_pass = sum(1 for b, a in zip(before, after) if b == 0 and a == 1)

    n = b_pass_a_fail + b_fail_a_pass
    if n == 0:
        return {"p_value": 1.0, "significant": False, "note": "No discordant pairs"}

    # Continuity-corrected McNemar statistic
    chi2_stat = (abs(b_pass_a_fail - b_fail_a_pass) - 1) ** 2 / n
    p_value = 1 - chi2.cdf(chi2_stat, df=1)
    return {
        "p_value": round(p_value, 4),
        "significant": p_value < 0.05,
        "regressions": b_pass_a_fail,
        "improvements": b_fail_a_pass,
    }

# p_value < 0.05 means the run-over-run change is unlikely to be noise

A p-value below 0.05 does not mean the regression is large — it means it is real. A regression from 90% to 87% on a 500-case suite can be statistically significant and still be operationally acceptable. Combine significance with effect size before deciding to block a deployment.
Step 4 — Root Cause Analysis
Once you have identified the failing slice, read the actual model outputs on the lowest-scoring cases. This is the step most teams skip and the step that provides the most information.
Clustering Failure Modes

Do not read every failing case individually — cluster them first. Cases that share a common failure mode will share surface features: the same entity name, the same input length range, the same instruction that the model misinterpreted.
Distinguishing Grader Failures from Model Failures

Not every low score means the model failed. The grader can produce false negatives — marking a valid output as incorrect. Before attributing a failure to the model, check a sample of low-scoring cases manually:
Scenario
What You See
Correct Interpretation
Model hallucinated
Output contains false claim; grader correctly scores 0
Model failure — fix prompt or add examples
Grader too strict
Output is valid paraphrase; exact-match grader scores 0
Grader failure — add normalization or switch to LLM judge
Gold label wrong
Model output is correct; human label was wrong
Dataset error — correct the label
Task underspecified
Multiple valid outputs exist; only one was labeled
Suite design issue — use rubric-based grading

The fastest way to distinguish grader from model failure is to read the lowest-scoring 10 cases yourself and score them. If your human score disagrees with the grader on more than 3 of 10, the grader has a systematic error.
Tracking Score Trends Over Time
A single run is a snapshot. Trends across multiple runs reveal whether the system is improving, stable, or drifting — and flag when grader changes or suite mutations introduce discontinuities.

import sqlite3, json
from datetime import datetime

def plot_score_trend(conn: sqlite3.Connection, model: str) -> list[dict]:
    rows = conn.execute(
        """SELECT suite_hash, score, n, timestamp
           FROM runs
           WHERE model = ?
           ORDER BY timestamp ASC""",
        (model,)
    ).fetchall()

    trend = []
    prev_hash = None
    for hash_, score, n, ts in rows:
        entry = {
            "date": datetime.fromtimestamp(ts).strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
            "score": score,
            "n": n,
            "suite_changed": hash_ != prev_hash,
        }
        trend.append(entry)
        prev_hash = hash_

    # Flag suite-change discontinuities — these break trend lines
    for i, entry in enumerate(trend):
        if entry["suite_changed"] and i > 0:
            entry["note"] = "Suite hash changed — not comparable to previous run"
    return trend
Mark suite hash changes as discontinuities in your trend chart. A score jump that coincides with a suite hash change is not evidence of model improvement — it could be that hard cases were removed.
Acting on Results
Interpretation is not complete until it leads to a decision. Three outcomes are possible:
Ship.
Score meets threshold, no significant regression in any slice, and sub-group analysis shows no critical slice below its floor. Document the run hash and score as the new baseline.
Fix and re-run.

A specific failure pattern is identified — wrong tone on formal cases, hallucination on long inputs. Fix the prompt, add few-shot examples, or adjust the system instruction. Re-run the eval and verify the target slice improves without regressing others.
Investigate further.

The regression is real but the root cause is unclear after reading 10 failing cases. Expand the investigation: run on additional sub-groups, add more tagged cases around the suspicious slice, or involve a domain expert to read outputs.

The loop between an eval result and a prompt change should be tight — ideally under a day. Long gaps between eval runs and prompt updates allow interpretation fatigue to set in, where teams start accepting below-threshold scores because they no longer remember what the threshold was for.
Confidence Intervals on Aggregate Scores

A score of 88% on a 100-case suite could realistically range from 81% to 94% if you ran it 100 times with a different random draw of cases. Reporting a single number without a confidence interval overstates precision.
For binary (pass/fail) graders, the Wilson score interval gives a reliable 95% CI even for small samples:

import math

def wilson_ci(successes: int, n: int, z: float = 1.96) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """95% confidence interval on a proportion using the Wilson score method."""
    if n == 0:
        return (0.0, 1.0)
    p_hat = successes / n
    denominator = 1 + z**2 / n
    centre = (p_hat + z**2 / (2 * n)) / denominator
    margin = z * math.sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / n + z**2 / (4 * n**2)) / denominator
    return (round(max(0, centre - margin), 4), round(min(1, centre + margin), 4))

# Example: 88 passes out of 100 cases
lo, hi = wilson_ci(88, 100)
print(f"Score: 88.0% (95% CI: {lo*100:.1f}% - {hi*100:.1f}%)")
# Score: 88.0% (95% CI: 80.1% - 93.4%)

# The interval widens dramatically with fewer cases:
lo50, hi50 = wilson_ci(44, 50)  # same 88% on 50 cases
print(f"Score: 88.0% (95% CI: {lo50*100:.1f}% - {hi50*100:.1f}%) on 50 cases")
# Score: 88.0% (95% CI: 76.5% - 94.7%)

When the confidence intervals of two runs overlap, you cannot conclude that one is better than the other — even if the point estimates differ. This is the statistical reason that +3pp on a 100-case suite is in the noise zone.
Communicating Results to Stakeholders
Raw eval scores mean little to product managers and business stakeholders. Translate scores into business terms before sharing:
Eval Score
Business Translation
How to Frame
88% on billing intent classification
12% of billing queries misrouted
Revenue impact: X misdirected tickets per day
Score improved +5pp
Failure rate dropped by nearly half on that slice
Fewer escalations expected per week
Adversarial pass rate 45%
More than half of injection attempts succeed
Security risk: requires immediate prompt hardening
Regression -3pp on long inputs
Long-context queries degrade with new model
Enterprise customers most affected
Frame scores in terms of user-facing outcomes. A 3-percentage-point swing on an eval is hard to interpret; '30 additional failed queries per 1,000' is not.
The Results Summary Format
A one-page results summary for stakeholders should contain exactly four things:
Overall score vs threshold and vs last run (two numbers, one sentence each)
Worst-performing slice and its pass rate (one sentence with a recommendation)
Whether any regression is statistically significant (yes/no with a brief explanation)
Recommended action: ship, fix-and-rerun, or investigate
Everything else — sub-group tables, McNemar statistics, raw case outputs — belongs in an appendix for engineers who need to trace the decision.
Distinguishing Systematic from Random Failures

Two cases failing is noise. Ten cases failing in the same way is a pattern. The distinction matters because systematic failures require prompt changes while random failures often reflect grader variance or underspecified tasks.
A feature that appears in 80% of failing cases but only 20% of passing cases is a strong signal for a systematic failure mode. A feature that appears equally in both groups is a coincidence.
Common Interpretation Mistakes
Mistake
Why It Misleads
Correction
Reading score without suite hash check
A suite change can inflate score without any model improvement
Always verify suite hash before comparing runs
Shipping on +2pp improvement
Within noise range for suites under 300 cases
Require +5pp or statistical significance
Ignoring sub-group scores
Critical slice failure hidden by majority-class performance
Report worst sub-group score alongside overall
Attributing all failures to model
Grader false negatives inflate failure counts
Manually review 10 low-score cases before debugging
Comparing scores across grader versions
LLM judge prompt changes produce score deltas
Lock judge model and rubric as part of the suite version
Treating CI eval as the only signal
CI suite too small to detect slow drift
Run full suite nightly; CI suite for fast regression gate only
Summary

A score is the start of interpretation, not the end. Work through four layers before acting: confirm the suite and grader are unchanged, break down sub-group scores to find the failing slice, apply a statistical test to confirm the regression is real, and read failing cases to identify the root cause.
Check suite hash and grader version before comparing any two runs
Always compute sub-group pass rates — an 88% overall score can hide a 30% adversarial pass rate
Use McNemar's test on binary scores to distinguish real regressions from sampling noise
Manually review the 10 lowest-scoring cases before attributing failure to the model vs the grader
Track suite hash changes as trend discontinuities — they invalidate direct score comparisons
